title: Dog Broken Tooth with Exposed Pulp: Symptoms & Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out if your dog's broken tooth with exposed pulp is an emergency. Learn why it's painful, how to triage, and why you need to call a vet today.
content_html:
What is Exposed Pulp?

When a dog's tooth is fractured severely enough to reveal the pulp, it means the innermost chamber containing sensitive nerves and blood vessels is open to the environment. You might notice a small pink or red dot in the center of the broken surface, which may eventually turn brown or black as the tissue dies. This condition is significantly more serious than a simple chip of the enamel.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a broken tooth with exposed pulp is Medium. While this is rarely a life-threatening situation, it is an acutely painful one. Because the pulp is exposed, bacteria from the mouth can travel directly into the jawbone, leading to infection and dental abscesses. You should seek veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours to manage the pain and prevent further complications.

How a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the affected tooth can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. A photo allows the vet to determine the exact type of fracture and confirm if the pulp cavity is truly involved. This information helps the clinic decide how quickly your pet needs to be fit into the schedule and allows them to prepare a more accurate treatment plan, such as a root canal or an extraction.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain medication for a broken tooth?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your dog human medications like ibuprofen or acetaminophen, as these are toxic to pets. Your vet will prescribe safe, pet-specific pain relief.

faqs.question: Will my dog stop eating if the tooth is painful?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Many dogs will continue to eat despite intense dental pain. Look for subtle signs like dropping food, chewing on one side of the mouth, or pawing at the face.

faqs.question: What are the common treatment options for pulp exposure?
faqs.answer: The two primary treatments are a root canal (to save the tooth structure) or a surgical extraction (to remove the source of pain and infection).
